text: The robotic view shows the surgical field where lymph nodes have been dissected and removed from around the external iliac artery and vein, as well as the obturator nerve. Node packets are visible on both sides, prepared for later bagging. The bladder remains elevated to facilitate visualization of the seminal vesicles (SVs), which are approached via posterior dissection.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection, making an incision approximately one centimeter above the junction of yellow perirectal fat and the white peritoneal reflection on the bladder. The dissection proceeds through a thin layer of tissue, leading to the Denonvilliers' fascia. The vas deferens is identified and lifted, revealing the seminal vesicles below.

The surgeon places the patient in stirrups and begins the dissection of the left seminal vesicle (SV). The SV is lifted and manipulated, following its medial aspect around the corner to the tip. The surgeon then pulls the SV down towards the contralateral shoulder to expose the lateral aspect for further dissection.

The surgeon performs dissection along the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicle (SV), ensuring close adherence to the SV wall without using clips. The fourth robotic arm is adjusted to provide adequate retraction, facilitating clear visualization and access to the SV, even in obese patients.

The surgeon demonstrates the dissection technique by making an incision one centimeter above the line where the yellow perirectal fat meets the white peritoneal reflection. The dissection proceeds by staying in the 'hay' layer down to the Denonvilliers' fascia, avoiding lateral dissection to prevent encountering the ureter. The anatomy is consistently found in the midline, and the surgeon emphasizes staying above the Denonvilliers' fascia to avoid the rectum.

The surgeon carefully follows the contour of the seminal vesicle (SV), adjusting retraction to maintain a clear view and avoid working in a confined space. The SV is hugged closely during dissection, with retraction adjustments made as needed to accommodate variations in SV size.

The surgeon works on the posterior bladder neck with a partially dropped bladder, using a pneumo of six with the air seal system. The surgical field appears tighter compared to higher pneumo pressures, as the team performs the procedure under ultra-low pressure conditions.

The surgeon opens the endopelvic fascia to better visualize the contour of the prostate gland, aiding in identifying the bladder neck. The fascia is carefully dissected to enhance exposure before proceeding with the bladder neck dissection.

The surgeon prepares to take on the bladder neck, ensuring the pneumo pressure remains at six, despite previous concerns about bleeding during cold cutting of the DVC. The cautery settings are adjusted to a monopolar of four on the Irby or twenty-five on the regular Bovie machine.

The surgeon carefully dissects through the bladder neck, maintaining a low cautery setting to preserve the visibility of muscle fibers. The left hand provides backward traction to stretch the tissue, aiding in the clear definition of muscle fibers and preventing accidental entry into the prostate. The scissors float above the tissue, avoiding deep penetration to ensure precise dissection.

The surgeon encounters venous bleeding near the prostatic urethra, indicating proximity to the urethra. The Foley catheter is visible, with a black line drawn at the level of the eye hole to aid in positioning and rotation.

The surgeon uses a marking pen and continues the dissection of the bladder neck with a light touch of the cautery, avoiding digging into the tissue to minimize charring. The bladder neck fibers are followed closely, with the left hand providing traction to aid in the process. The surgeon prefers bladder neck sparing to facilitate a smoother anastomosis, aiming to match the size of the bladder neck with the urethra for a watertight closure.

The surgeon focuses on freeing the corners of the posterior bladder neck from the prostate, using the left hand to provide tension and exposure. Once the corners are freed, the surgeon cuts to maintain a tight bladder neck, ensuring it does not enlarge during the procedure.

The surgeon uses the left hand to provide traction on the bladder neck fibers, ensuring they are stretched and visible. The cautery is applied lightly to minimize charring, allowing the muscle fibers to contract visibly. The scissors are positioned to keep both tips exposed, facilitating precise dissection without losing sight of the tips.

The surgeon uses one prong of the scissor to cut, minimizing char, while switching to both prongs for coagulation when encountering a bleeder. The approach involves identifying the plane to reach the vas deferens and seminal vesicles, ensuring not to be too close to the prostate base. Fat tissue indicates the transition through the bladder muscle, guiding the search for the vas and seminal vesicles.

The surgeon is working in the rectal plane, opting to leave the Denonvilliers fascia on the prostate and dissecting into the perirectal fat plane. An incision is made in the lateral prostatic fascia, with a less aggressive approach to nerve sparing due to the patient's lack of sexual activity.

The surgeon defines the lateral edge of the prostate by dissecting around a vein, revealing the bottom edge of the prostate where it starts to turn down towards the perirectal fat. The Denonvias tissue is clipped separately to thin out the pedicle, using robotic clip appliers for precise placement.

The surgeon places clips on the pedicle during the prostatectomy, ensuring they are positioned cranially to avoid interference with the future anastomosis at the apex. The clips are carefully applied to prevent erosion into the bladder. The surgeon discusses the use of vessel sealers and the cost considerations involved.

The surgeon places clips primarily at the pedicle, avoiding distal placement near the apex, and focuses on the neurovascular bundle. The posterolateral edge of the prostate is clearly visible, with careful dissection to avoid clipping nerves prematurely. The pedicle is taken close to the prostate to preserve nerve integrity, ensuring the nerves can follow the posterolateral aspect of the gland.

The surgeon incises the Denonvias tissue left on the prostate to facilitate nerve sparing, creating a square of Denonvias on the posterior side of the prostate. The anterior base tumor in the anterior transition zone allows for a close nerve-sparing approach, as the patient's recent surveillance biopsy was benign.

The surgeon makes an incision on the medial side of the prostate, followed by blunt dissection on the lateral aspect near the neurovascular bundle. The neurovascular bundle is visible, and the surgeon carefully navigates around veins to avoid excessive bleeding. The prostate's edge is identified where it begins to turn, adjacent to the perirectal fat, guiding the approach to the pedicle.

The surgeon carefully dissects the neurovascular bundle from the prostate, using a fenestrated Maryland bipolar instrument for precise cautery. The dissection plane on one side is stickier due to multiple previous biopsies, while the other side appears more native. Despite the challenges, the nerve-sparing procedure is completed successfully.

The surgeon carefully cuts closer to the prostate to preserve urethral length while excising tissue from the dorsal venous complex (DVC). Despite some bleeding, it is not clinically significant, and the patient will not require a transfusion. The technique allows for wider excision without compromising the urethral length, especially beneficial for patients with anterior base tumors.

The surgeon uses an assistant stapler to cut the DVC, leaving more tissue on the prostate side to preserve urethral length. The catheter is reinserted, and the posterior urethra is cut before addressing the posterior lip to avoid leaving any prostate tissue behind.

The surgeon adjusts the urethral length to ensure it remains unchanged while widening the dissection area to achieve better margins. The nerve-sparing technique is employed, keeping the pedicles tight to the prostate to avoid clipping the neurovascular bundle. Clips are placed distal to the node of Cloquet to prevent lymphatic backflow, using bipolar cautery for sealing lymphatic vessels and minimizing monopolar use to reduce lymphoceles.

The surgeon performs a posterior reconstruction stitch by suturing the edge of the nonanvase behind the urethra, incorporating peritoneum near the seminal vesicles and posterior bladder tissue. A slider knot is used to bring these tissues together, aligning the anastomosis and reducing tension. The Stratafix barbed suture is utilized for the anastomosis, starting at approximately four o'clock on the clock face, ensuring the backside is watertight.

The surgeon completes the posterior wall of the anastomosis using a V-Lock suture, ensuring it is watertight from approximately four to seven o'clock. The technique involves starting at seven o'clock to avoid leaks at six o'clock, and the V-Lock suture helps reduce tension during the procedure.

The surgeon places a piece of Vicryl as a pledget to prevent the knot from tearing through the bladder during the anastomosis. Using a Stratafix suture, the surgeon carefully stitches deep into the corner, securing the posterior urethra and surrounding tissue to prevent leaks. The left hand is used to stabilize the back wall of the urethra during the procedure, while venous oozing is managed with suction.
The surgeon clips the peritoneum to close the lateral twelve port, preventing potential herniation of the appendix into the surgical site. The prostatectomy procedure is completed.
